subject: Astronomy & Space
title: Life in Boiling Water
description: A 60-second lesson on Life in Boiling Water.

hook.text: Deep-sea hydrothermal vents blast water at over 300°C. Yet microbes thrive there, fueled by chemical energy, not sunlight.

buildup.text: These organisms use chemosynthesis—harvesting energy from hydrogen sulfide and other chemicals from Earth's interior.

discovery.text: The first vent ecosystems were discovered in 1977 near the Galápagos. Giant tube worms and bacteria stunned biologists.

twist.text: Some scientists believe life itself may have originated at hydrothermal vents—making them the cradle of all biology.

climax.text: If life can start at vents on Earth, similar vents on Europa or Enceladus could support life right now.

punchline.text: Life thriving at 300°C in total darkness. No sunlight needed. Just chemistry.

quiz.question: Why are hydrothermal vents relevant to the search for extraterrestrial life?
options: Similar vents may exist on ocean moons like Europa and Enceladus, supporting life without sunlight
They prove that life can only form in extremely hot environments
Vents produce the only type of atmosphere that supports complex life
